title: Veterinary Services

narrative.executive_view: Veterinary Services is a conditional lower-middle-market roll-up: AI can remove meaningful documentation and front-desk work, while the paid clinical product remains tied to licensed, physical care. The important discipline is not to mistake an AI scribe for replacement of a DVM, technician, or animal-care task. The code also includes reference laboratories and specialty hospitals, so this packet is framed around a multi-doctor companion-animal clinic rather than an average establishment. [S1][S2][S3]
narrative.how_ai_changes_work: The near-term workflow is ambient documentation, record drafting, follow-up messages, scheduling, reminder handling, refill routing, and revenue-cycle administration. AAHA describes AI scribes as tools that create records from veterinarian-client conversations and emphasizes the need to test accuracy; the specialty survey reports routine AI use but also a low self-reported knowledge base. That supports gradual, supervised adoption rather than unattended clinical substitution. [S3][S4]
narrative.value_capture: A clinic has a direct client relationship and mostly clinic-set fee schedule, so some saved administrative time can remain in margin or be reinvested in appointment capacity. That retention is incomplete: staff scarcity, software subscriptions, local competition, and client price sensitivity can absorb savings. Low insurance penetration does not prove unlimited pricing power, but it does mean the standard human-health insurer cost-control channel is limited; NAPHIA reports 6.4 million insured U.S. pets at year-end 2024. [S6][S7]
narrative.adoption_timing: Effective adoption should reach half of relevant administrative and documentation workflows in roughly three years, not immediately. The cited survey is a specialty-membership sample rather than a representative GP-practice census: 42% of ACVIM respondents and 33% of ECVIM-CA respondents routinely used AI, with scribes the most common tool. Clinic implementation also requires PIMS integration, privacy review, clinician verification, and staff training. [S3][S4]
narrative.consolidation_economics: Buyability is real but the deterministic band count is conservative: it captures only the 100-499 employee class, whereas many practical clinic targets have lower EBITDA after replacing owner-DVM labor. AAHA, citing Brakke, reports substantial corporate ownership and names Mars-owned Banfield, BluePearl, and VCA as major platforms. For multiples, the directly reopened market evidence is a 3.44x median asking-price/SDE multiple for small listed veterinary practices, not an adjusted-EBITDA sale multiple; this packet therefore uses a conservative SDE-to-EBITDA proxy and no expansion thesis. [S5][S7]
narrative.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is durable for the clinical core. FDA says prescription animal drugs can be dispensed only by or on the lawful order of a licensed veterinarian, and VCPR requirements apply to important prescribing contexts. AI can triage, document, and assist decisions, but it cannot itself perform surgery, examinations, handling, or the accountable professional role. The residual risk is that AI deflects some low-acuity questions and that telemedicine rules evolve at the state level. [S2]
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The central risks are owner-DVM key-person dependence, a chronic clinical-labor constraint, the gap between a specialty AI survey and general-practice adoption, and mismatched valuation bases. The selection excludes a claim that every veterinary act requires an in-person visit: FDA notes state rules matter. It also treats the supplied target-band estimate as a screen rather than a count of the actual clinic acquisition universe. [S2][S4][S7]

sources.title: 2022 NAICS 541940 Veterinary Services
sources.what_it_supports: Official scope: licensed veterinary medical, dental, surgical, and veterinary testing establishments; it also distinguishes nonveterinary pet care.

sources.title: FDA: Veterinarian-Client-Patient Relationships, Prescribing/Dispensing Animal Drugs and Telemedicine
sources.what_it_supports: Federal licensed-veterinarian and VCPR requirements for prescription and extralabel drug contexts, with state-specific rules potentially adding requirements.

sources.title: AAHA: Generative AI scribing tools: Considerations for implementation in veterinary practice
sources.what_it_supports: Current veterinary use case for AI-assisted medical-record drafting and the need to manage accuracy risks.

sources.title: Use of, and attitudes toward, artificial intelligence in veterinary internal medicine
sources.what_it_supports: Survey evidence that 42% of ACVIM and 33% of ECVIM-CA respondents routinely used AI, most commonly AI-enhanced medical scribes, plus stated selection-bias limitations.

sources.title: AAHA: Corporate consolidation and the rise of private equity
sources.what_it_supports: Brakke-attributed 2021 corporate-ownership estimates and examples of major veterinary consolidators; it is contextual rather than a current transaction database.

sources.title: NAPHIA 2025 State of the Industry Report release
sources.what_it_supports: NAPHIA-reported 6,405,541 insured U.S. pets at year-end 2024 and the association's market-coverage description.

sources.title: BizBuySell Veterinary Practices for Sale market overview
sources.what_it_supports: Reopened listing-market data: 53 listings, median 3.44x seller's discretionary earnings asking multiple, and a 2.82x-4.10x listed range; it is not closed-sale EBITDA evidence.

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: Weight the supplied code-specific SOC mix by task, not headcount: physical and licensed clinical work is mostly augmented, while communications, documentation, scheduling, and back-office work are materially automatable. The resulting share is an estimate of replaceable task cost, not of eliminated clinical positions.
caveats: The estimate treats AI scribes as documentation augmentation and caps DVM substitution well below administrative work.
The supplied mix is 5-digit 54194 and includes laboratories and specialty care as well as GP clinics; role-level fractions are a companion-clinic investment view.

role_judgments.rationale: Technician core work is physical handling, monitoring, draws, and positioning; records and routine communications are the small automatable part.
Assistant and animal-care work is predominantly physical animal handling and care, with limited record support automation.
AI can draft notes and assist information retrieval, but licensed diagnosis, prescribing oversight, and procedures remain human work.
Scheduling, reminders, intake, and routine messages are suited to phone, text, and workflow automation; in-person flow remains.
Animal-care tasks are overwhelmingly on-site physical work.
Routine status, reminder, routing, and FAQ contacts can be automated, while sensitive conversations and escalation remain human.
Scheduling, reports, and payroll preparation automate; supervision and exception handling remain.
Records requests, document preparation, and routine data entry are highly automatable with review.
KPI reporting and scheduling optimization automate, but people management and service recovery remain.
Reconciliations, invoicing, and routine bookkeeping work are highly automatable with accountable review.
The unlisted wage share likely mixes specialist clinical, diagnostic, and administrative tasks, so use a middle, explicitly uncertain fraction.

pi_dist.rationale: Direct client billing and the relatively limited insured-pet base support partial retention of admin savings, but local competition and growing insurance constrain full retention.
caveats: Insured-pet counts are not a clinic-level payer-mix measure and do not establish local pricing power.

pi_moat.rationale: Licensed clinical responsibility, VCPR-linked work, client records, and local care access support a moderate moat, while corporate competitors and doctor turnover limit it.
caveats: Federal VCPR rules do not create a universal in-person requirement; state law and the service line matter.

t50_years.rationale: Specialty respondents show meaningful but sub-50% routine use, while practice integration and accuracy controls slow effective rollout across the broader clinic workflow.
caveats: The survey is a voluntary specialty-membership sample, not a representative general-practice adoption census.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: Use a rounded midpoint of the reopened 42% ACVIM and 33% ECVIM-CA routine-use rates as a directional starting point below 50%.
caveats: This is a specialty-survey proxy, not a measured adoption rate for all NAICS 541940 operators.

historical_analogs.value: Practice-management software and electronic records show that regulated clinics can digitize workflow while retaining clinician review; AI scribing is the next workflow layer rather than autonomous care.
historical_analogs.rationale: The analogy is qualitative and limited to the adoption pattern, not a causal forecast of savings.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: Gradual workflow-tool adoption with clinician review
caveats: No audited historical AI adoption curve for independent GP clinics was found in the reopened sources.

selection.rationale: Use a cautious succession-screen assumption for independent clinic owners in a consolidating market rather than claiming an unobserved code-specific age statistic.
caveats: No reopened source provided a current national 541940 owner-over-60 percentage; diligence must replace this estimate with target-level owner age and succession intent.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: The documented presence of well-funded corporate buyers and practice ownership transitions supports a succession constraint, but not a quantified shortage rate.
caveats: Corporate consolidation evidence is not itself proof that each owner lacks an internal successor.

active_consolidators.rationale: Count a minimum set of active national/platform buyers represented by the major corporate groups discussed in the reopened consolidation source, rather than infer a complete buyer universe.
caveats: This is a screening count, not a verified current deal pipeline or a count of all buyers.

buy_mult.rationale: Anchor purchase underwriting to the reopened 3.44x median listed SDE multiple and round to 3.5x as a conservative small-practice proxy.
caveats: The source reports asking-price multiples of seller's discretionary earnings, not closed transactions or adjusted EBITDA; owner-DVM replacement must be normalized in diligence.

exit_mult.rationale: Assume no multiple expansion and reuse the same conservative small-practice SDE proxy at exit.
caveats: The exit multiple is not supported as an EBITDA platform multiple; it deliberately avoids an expansion thesis.

pricing_structure.value: Clinic-set fee-for-service with direct client payment and growing reimbursement by pet insurers
pricing_structure.rationale: Listing-market descriptions state that veterinary practices often bill clients directly, while NAPHIA documents a growing insured-pet base.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Mostly direct-pay; payer mix varies by clinic and geography
caveats: Neither source measures a specific target's realized payer mix or price elasticity.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: The labor uplift is concentrated in administrative and documentation tasks and assumes a three-year effective-adoption path, not instant clinical labor removal.

terminal_value.justification: Licensed, physical diagnosis, treatment, surgery, and prescribing oversight preserve paid clinical demand even as AI makes supporting workflow cheaper; low-acuity triage remains a material edge risk.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: No proxy was used for deterministic labor share, role weights, target counts, or factor scores. The specialty AI survey, market listing multiples, and consolidation evidence are disclosed proxies for their specific score inputs.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: AI may reduce administrative cost but clinic competition, subscriptions, and client price sensitivity can pass savings through; the capture inputs therefore retain only partial savings.

dataset_mismatch.description: The deterministic n_band only counts the 100-499 employee class under a generic margin assumption, while many clinic acquisition candidates are smaller and owner-DVM labor must be normalized.
sensitivity: B changes if 20-99 employee clinics qualify after target-specific margin and owner-compensation normalization.
M is sensitive to SDE versus adjusted-EBITDA basis.

market_boundary.selection_basis: This is the intended lower-middle-market acquisition operator; Census confirms the code also contains laboratory and specialty activities, which are not used as average-clinic evidence.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A faster rollout raises A but does not change the clinical task floor.
B is highly sensitive to verified seller age and actual succession intent.
M remains neutral here, but returns are sensitive to correct owner-compensation normalization.

reviewer_note: Every score-driving URL in S1-S7 was reopened on the access date. Listing-SDE and specialty-survey proxies are deliberately labeled and should receive target-level diligence.
